Us](https://sabreen.com/contact-us/)\n\n![Image 2: Adhesive Bonding & Joining - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/eddeb4a3a060b4203d96a3f31ff2ead6)\n\nAdhesive Bonding & Joining\n--------------------------\n\n### Adhesive Failure, Plastics Adhesion Bonding\n\n[Onsite Plasma Testing & Surface Science Education](https://indd.adobe.com/view/3ffb37c9-a56e-49a9-bde7-18b8c498ac35)\n\nAdhesive failures and adhesion failures occur at the interface of two similar or dissimilar substrate surfaces. Bonding processes involve both adhesive and cohesive forces. The type of adhesion failure can be characterized by adhesive, cohesive, or substrate failure, depending on the strength of these forces. Adhesive failure, or delamination, generally is caused by the characteristics of the substrate, including its surface chemistry and surface energy, surface preparation and application procedures. Cohesive failure relates to the internal strength of the adhesive. Substrate failure is not related to the bonding process, rather the strength of the substrate itself. The bond is stronger than the actual substrate.\n\nAdhesion problems can be rapidly solved with process engineering experts skilled in the fields of polymers and metals, chemistry, adhesives, primers, coatings, silanes, plasma pretreatments, surface quality, molding operations, and testing methods.\n\nThe Sabreen Group achieves outstanding success solving complex 2-dimensional and 3-dimensional bonding problems using our advanced technologies and Six Sigma process methods. We develop innovative solutions for a broad spectrum of applications, including jet aircraft fighters, medical, automotive, electronics, consumer products, and more. One of our patented processes was employed to manufacture \u201csmart bomb\u201d weapon systems during Operation Desert Storm and continues to be used as a standard of bonding excellence for military and NASA applications. Another technology enables exceptional waterborne coating adhesion performance on polyolefin products for exterior housing applications.\n\nSABEEN\u2019s technologies are employed to manufacture many of today\u2019s most recognizable military and commercial products, from the International Space Station and pharmaceuticals to underwater action cameras. SABREEN\u2019S engineering has earned top industry awards for our clients, including pharmaceutical/medical packaging product of the year, inducted into the Smithsonian National Museum. Our early project involvement achieves accelerated project schedules.\n\n### SABREEN's innovative solutions for applications include:\n\n*   Adhesives & Sealants\n*   Plastic to Plastic\n*   Elastomers & Rubbers\n*   Inkjet & Printing Inks\n*   Coatings & Paints\n*   Metallization & Plating\n\n### Custom bonding & curing technologies for hermetic sealing of polyolefins\n\n### Custom pretreatment, inkjet chemistry & UVLED Cure\n\n### Plasma pretreatment & custom ink on PPS lock safe dial\n\n### Breakthrough inkjet technology for elastomeric wearables\n\n### Optomechanical bonding anti-fog/AR/hardcoat on Polycarbonate\n\n### Plasma pretreatment for polyolefin closures\n\n### Flame lamination for automotive seating\n\n### Award winning pharma/medical fashionable birth control carrying cases\n\n### Polymer roofing tiles decorated/coated to simulate slate, cedar shake, 40-year warranty lightfastness, fire & wind\n\n### Full color digital printing with FDA chemical resistant clear topcoat for dishwasher stability\n\n### Custom decoration & special-effect top coatings simulate soft-touch & woodgrain\n\n Contact Angle  Surface Energy, Wetting  Chemical Surface Activation  Factors Influencing Adhesion & Shelf-life Aging  Selecting a Plasma Surface Pretreatment  Surface Cleanliness  Dyne Solutions Disadvantages  Failure Mechanisms of Adhesive Bonding  Surface Roughness  Data Surface Energy Polymers  Adhesives & Curing Using Structural Epoxies  Nylon Adhesion Bonding Process Methods  Adhesion Problems Due to Mold Release & Plasticizers  Techniques for Measuring Adhesion & Abrasion Durability \n\nThe contact angle is the angle typically measured through the liquid, where a liquid-vapor interface meets a solid interface. The contact angle quantifies the wettability of a solid surface by a liquid via the Young equation.\n\n![Image 3: Contact Angle - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/6670f821e2807e28ea5a4d8a7abb4d7c)\n\n![Image 4: Contact Angle - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/6670f821e2807e28ea5a4d8a7abb4d7c)\n\nThe underlying reasons why many plastics are difficult to bond are because they are hydrophobic non-polar materials, chemically inert and possess poor surface wettability \u2013 i.e., low surface energy. While these hydrophobic (repel water) performance properties are ideal for part designers who seek such properties, they are the nemesis for manufacturers needing to bond such materials. Robust adhesion bonding usually necessitates \u201chydrophilic\u201d surfaces. For optimum adhesion to occur, an adhesive (coating, ink or paint) must thoroughly \u201cwet out\u201d the surface (adherend) to be bonded.\n\n\u201cWetting out\u201d means the liquid flows and covers a surface to maximize the contact area and the attractive forces between the adhesive and adherend bonding surface. For a liquid to effectively wet out a surface, the surface energy of the adhesive must be as low or lower than the surface energy of the adherend to be bonded. Alternatively, the surface energy of the substrate must be raised.\n\nConsider a single liquid fluid droplet on a flat solid surface at rest (equilibrium). The angle formed by the solid surface and the tangent line to the upper surface at the end-point is called the contact angle; it is the angle (?) through the liquid between the tangent line at the contact point and the horizontal line of the solid surface. The bubble/droplet shape is due to the molecular forces by which all liquids, through contraction of the surface, tend to form the contained volume into a shape having the least surface area. The intermolecular forces that contract the surface are termed \u201csurface tension.\u201d Surface tension, a measurement of surface energy, is expressed in dynes/cm (SI N/m).\n\nThe higher the surface energy of the solid substrate relative to the surface tension of a liquid (water, printing inks, adhesives/encapsulation, coatings, etc.), the better will be its \u201cwettability\u201d and the smaller will be the contact angle (Figure 1 on page 2). As a rule, acceptable bonding adhesion is achieved when the surface energy of a substrate is approximately 8 to 10 dynes/cm greater than the surface tension of the liquid.\n\n![Image 5: Surface Energy, Wetting - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/ea13d94f7c8dbce3c5bf340814b7feb7)\n\n\u201cSurface wetting\u201d with hydrophilic vs. hydrophobic surfaces\n\nThere is a strong tendency for manufacturers to focus only on contact angles or other wettability measurements as the sole predictor for bonding problems and for conducting routine surface testing. Chemical surface functionality is equally important, whereby hydrophobic surfaces are activated into bondable hydrophilic surfaces. Gas-phase, \u201cglow-discharge,\u201d surface oxidation pretreatment processes are used for chemical surface activation. These processes are characterized by their ability to generate \u201cgas plasma,\u201d an extremely reactive gas consisting of free electrons, positive ions and other species. Plasmas often can be described as a fourth state of matter. As energy is supplied, solids melt into liquids, liquids vaporize into gases, and gases ionize into \u201cplasmas.\u201d\n\nIn the science of physics, the mechanisms in which these plasmas are generated are different, but their effects on surface wettability are similar. The basic chemical and physical reaction that occurs is free electrons, ions, metastables, radicals and UV generated in the plasma can impact a surface with energies sufficient to break the molecular bonds on the surface of most polymeric substrates. This creates very reactive free radicals on the polymer surface that, in turn, can form, cross-link, or in the presence of oxygen, react rapidly to form various chemical functional groups on the substrate surface. Polar functional groups that can form and enhance bondability include carbonyl (C=O), carboxyl (HOOC), hydroperoxide (HOO-), and hydroxyl (HO-) groups. Even small amounts of reactive functional groups incorporated into polymers can be highly beneficial to improving surface chemical functionality and wettability. Also, chemical primers/solvents and mechanical abrasion (including mold tool texture) can be utilized alone or in conjunction with pretreatments.\n\nThe degree or quality of pretreatment for robust adhesion strength is affected by the cleanliness of the plastic surfaces. The surface must be clean to achieve optimal pretreatment and subsequent adhesion. Contamination sources on product surfaces that inhibit treatment includes dirt, dust, grease and oil. Low molecular weight materials such as silicones, mold release and anti-slip agents are particularly deleterious for bonding. Material purity is also an important factor. Further, certain soluble or nonsoluble compound agents used in pigment and dye colorants can adversely affect adhesion.\n\nThe shelf life (treatment aging degradation) of treated plastics depends on the type of resin, formulation and the ambient environment of the storage area. Shelf life of treated products is limited by the presence of low molecular weight oxidized materials (LMWOM) such as antioxidants, plasticizers, slip and antistatic agents, colorants and pigments, stabilizers, etc. Exposure of treated surfaces to elevated temperatures increases molecular chain mobility. The higher the chain mobility is, the faster the aging of the pretreatment. Plasma-treated surfaces age at different rates and to varying extents relative to factors with the surrounding environment. Aging characteristics and their storage shelf life are essential to manufacturing process operations. Activated surfaces may have a shelf life of hours, days, months or longer. It is recommended to bond, coat, paint or decorate products as soon as possible following pretreatment.\n\nRecognize that each surface pretreatment method is application-specific and may possess unique advantages and potential limitations. For robust results, consider the following factors:\n\n*   Polymers react differently to oxidation processes. The type of polymeric substrate and its end-use performance requirements are critical in determining the selection of pretreatment method.\n*   Is the substrate (product to be treated) conductive? For example, unassembled plastic electronic connector bodies \u2013 without metal contact pins \u2013 can be treated electrically, whereas assembled connectors may experience electrical arcing problems.\n*   Part geometry \u2013 flat surfaces are more easily treated compared to deep recesses, extreme tapers, and other shape irregularities. Wetting tests are difficult to conduct in small areas and on heavily textured surfaces.\n*   Material handling automation: Conductive belts and chains may cause electrical arcing with classical electrical corona discharge and spot treaters. Alternatively, consider using flame, cold gas plasma, or low-potential atmospheric plasma.\n*   Avoid overtreatment. Excessive plasma-oxidized surfaces may deleteriously affect downstream assembly processes such as heat sealing/welding.\n*   All pretreatment equipment is _not_ created equal. Examine the quality of constructed systems in action. For electrical treatment processes, observe the uniformity of the plasma discharge; for flame treatment, consider the differences between ribbon vs. drilled port burners and combustion system components; for cold gas plasma, examine the quality of the chamber construction, electrode shelves and particularly the manufacturer of the vacuum pump.\n\nBest method for inspecting surface quality \u2013 The Surface Analyst\u2122 2001 is a handheld digital device for measuring contact angles in-line production without using chemical dyne solutions. The method is nondestructive; thus the part can be processed as usual \u2013 unlike dyne solution testing in which the part is scraped. Invented by BTG Labs, the Surface Analyst\u2122 was engineered to put precision surface measurement technology in the hands of shop-floor technicians for operations where surface condition is critically important, such as adhesive bonding, painting, coating, printing, and decontamination.\n\nUsing a patented technique called \u201cballistic deposition,\u201d the device deposits a highly purified drop of water on the substrate surface. By knowing the volume and area of a drop of water, the device then calculates the contact angle of the water against a given surface. The device provides immediate feedback to the user with a quantifiable number or a Pass/Fail alert. The inspection data provided by the device allows users to determine if the substrate has been properly prepared for adhesion processes as a replacement for destructive dyne solutions. The handheld unit operates in any orientation and works on smooth or textured surfaces, such as grained TPOs. This data can be used for continuous process improvement, identifying quality drifts that may signal imminent problems, or to pinpoint the critical cause of product failures.\n\n![Image 6: Dyne Solutions Disadvantages - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/6138f3a929d941313d572c15ba95db88)\n\nBTG Labs Surface Analyst. Photos courtesy of BTG LABS (513.469.1800).\n\nFor many applications it may only be necessary to examine the static equilibrium contact angle using dyne solutions in accordance to a documented test procedure. Application kits or \u201cdyne pens/solutions\u201d provide useful information, but they are not precise measurements of surface tension. Surface tension measurements can vary considerably by individual (technique) and the interpretation of the \u201ccenter\u201d liquid behavior. Dyne pens/solutions are known to be directional indicators of significant differences in the surface tension and capable of identifying \u201cgood\u201d and \u201cbad\u201d bondable surfaces at economical pricing. Bottled dyne solutions can be preferable instead of pens because of contamination issues due to multiple usage.\n\nOptimal joint design is critical in any adhesive bonding application. Bonded joints can be subject to tensile, compressive, shear, peel or cleavage forces, often in combination. There are generally three distinctive types of bonding failure mechanisms:\n\n*   Adhesion failure interfacially between the adhesive and one of the adherends\n*   Cohesion failure by fracture of the adhesive layer leaving traces of adhesion on both surfaces\n*   Mixed-mode failure exhibits characteristics of both adhesion and cohesion failure\n\n![Image 7: Failure Mechanisms of Adhesive Bonding - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/eb467f1074717ce61d32fd1ce6e46bbe)\n\n_Adhesion failures_ occur at the interface between the adhesive and the adherend (substrate). Visually, there will be residual adhesive remaining at any location on only one surface. Adhesion failures that occur in-the-field almost always originate in the manufacturing process; materials, adhesives, surface preparation, contamination, procedures/workmanship, environment, quality control testing and combinations thereof. The remaining cause is interfacial degradation, as a function of field service life. This latter problem can be prevented during the product design/qualification phase.\n\n_Cohesion failures_ result in fracture of the adhesive and are characterized by the visual presence of the adhesive material on the matching faces of both adherends (substrates). Bonds which fail by cohesion exhibit high-strength performance (achieving optimal dry adhesive bond properties). The adhesive surface may appear \u201crough\u201d and may be lighter in color than the adhesive material, as applied. Failure is usually by shear, but peel stresses or a combination of shear and peel can produce cohesion failure. Cohesion failures that occur in-the-field are typically caused by poor joint design, e.g., insufficient overlap length, or excessive peel forces. Excessive porosity (humidity exposure), and the presence of voids below a critical size can also result in cohesion failure.\n\n_Mixed-mode failures_ exhibit characteristics of both adhesion failure and cohesion failure because the interface is partially degraded. Essentially, this failure mode is a transitional phase in which the strength of the adhesive bond is lower than the cohesion failure strength.\n\nSurface roughness is the degree of unevenness of the surface of a solid below the scale of magnitude of its shape or undulation but above the irregularity of crystal lattice structures. The degree of roughness affects the wettability of a solid. As roughness goes hand-in-hand with an enlarged surface area, it affects the wettability of a solid, the contact angle of a liquid, and the adhesion.\n\nRelative to the surface texture of the part to be bonded, as molded, a textured surface will increase mechanical interlocking adhesion in addition to solvent cleaning and plasma pretreatment. Texture can be accomplished within the mold tool or manually using a Scotch-Brite pad. Even slightly textured surfaces such as C-3 (320 Stone) will be beneficial. SPI Finish guide is below.\n\n![Image 8: SPI Finish Guide - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/2bc9ba31f29f334c1046e5d34cd75c56)\n\n![Image 9: Data Surface Polymers - The Sabreen Group, Inc.](blob:http://localhost/79d62e78224b7b59f2d28b6c1da36f40)\n\nFor many polymer applications, 2-component, heat curable structural epoxy adhesives are ideal. Two-component epoxy consists of mixing an epoxy resin plus a hardener or procuring \u201cduo-pak\u201d applicators. Uniform, thin bond line thickness (0.002 \u2013 0.007 inches) is preferred for optimal shear and tensile strength properties.\n\nFull cure time is typically in the range of seven (7) days at 75\u00b0F, but is variable between applications and environment conditions. The speed of cure can be accelerated by adding heat (120-210\u00b0F) which results in additional polymerization and can give the epoxy better properties. Bake time and temperature is application specific. One-component epoxies eliminate the need for mixing but cure only with the addition of heat (normally 250-300 \u00b0F). By comparison, the working life of two-component epoxies is limited.\n\nA common mispractice is to apply extra adhesive to improve bond strength. Excessive a"

The text establishes that untreated PPS has a surface energy of approximately 38 dynes/cm, requiring plasma pretreatment to reach 60-70 dynes/cm for robust bonding, exceeding the minimum 48-54 dynes/cm calculated for epoxy wetting. Methodologies include Electrical Corona Discharge, Atmospheric Blown Ion, Flame Plasma, and RF Cold Gas, with RF Cold Gas noted for hydrocarbon removal without pre-cleaning. This overlaps with the research question's focus on plasma-activated substrates, though the substrate here is PPS rather than polypropylene or aluminum oxide. Adhesive curing occurs at 300-350 degrees Fahrenheit, ensuring full crosslinking for heat-deflection temperatures above 500 degrees Fahrenheit. The article emphasizes that insufficient cure is a common failure mode, a variable critical when comparing experimental strengths to theoretical predictions. Glass-filled variants (30-40 percent) are discussed, introducing surface texture variables affecting mechanical interlocking. While the adhesive system is epoxy rather than PEI/DVS, the surface energy targets and plasma necessity provide a comparative baseline for activation intensity. Confidence in the plasma methodology is MEDIUM, as these are established industry practices, but confidence in relevance to the specific research question is LOW due to material mismatch. It adds understanding regarding the criticality of mold temperature (275-300 degrees Fahrenheit) on crystallization, which affects surface appearance and bonding. No molecular dynamics predictions are offered, limiting direct comparison to the research question's theoretical component. The industry perspective dominates, focusing on practical manufacturing constraints rather than fundamental bond physics.\n\n    (Word count check: ~250 words). The study reports adhesion strengths of 3.7 MPa on stainless steel, 3.1 MPa on glass, and 2.7 MPa on copper, while noting weaker forces on low surface energy substrates like polypropylene (PP) and PTFE. This directly addresses the substrate variable in the research question, confirming PP as a challenging substrate for adhesion without specific activation described here. The work combines experimental results with theoretical calculations, identifying strong interaction energy between POMs and polymer as the main factor for performance, paralleling the research question's interest in comparing measured strengths with molecular dynamics predictions. The adhesive tolerates temperatures from -196 to 55 degrees Celsius, exceeding commercial hot melt adhesives. Methodology includes FT-IR, PXRD, XPS, and NMR to confirm structure, with crosslink density measured at 1.55 × 10^-4 mol mL^-1 for the adhesive versus 69.93 × 10^-4 mol mL^-1 for PEG. Confidence in the quantitative adhesion data is HIGH, but relevance to DVS crosslinked PEI is LOW due to chemical differences. The study highlights that adhesion failure occurs mainly at the interface, indicating high cohesion interaction. This contrasts with the research question's focus on crosslinked PEI coatings, yet the methodological approach of validating theoretical interaction energy against macroscopic shear strength is highly relevant. The article adds understanding regarding the impact of crosslink density on adhesion performance and the viability of solvent-free processing. It does not mention divinyl sulfone or polyethylenimine, representing a significant chemical divergence. However, the demonstration that theoretical calculations can explain low-temperature tolerance offers a framework for how MD predictions might correlate with experimental lap-shear data in other systems. The emerging trend of solvent-free processing is noted as advantageous for scale-up.\n\n    (Word count check: ~280 words).     1: """
    2: OpenRouter LLM Client for polaris graph.
    3: 
    4: Single gateway to Qwen 3.5 Plus via OpenRouter. Two call modes:
    5: - reason(): reasoning ON, returned separately in reasoning_details
    6: - generate(): reasoning OFF, clean prose output only
    7: 
    8: No CoT scrubbing. No regex filters. No post-processing hacks.
    9: The API handles separation at the protocol level.
   10: """
   11: 
   12: import asyncio
   13: import json
   14: import logging
   15: import os
   16: import re
   17: import time
   18: from dataclasses import dataclass, field
   19: from datetime import datetime, timezone
   20: from pathlib import Path
   21: from typing import Any, Optional, Type, TypeVar
   22: 
   23: import httpx
   24: from dotenv import load_dotenv
   25: from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError
   26: 
   27: from src.polaris_graph.tracing import _current_tracer
   28: 
   29: load_dotenv()
   30: 
   31: logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
   32: 
   33: T = TypeVar("T", bound=BaseModel)
   34: 
   35: # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   36: # Configuration
   37: # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   38: 
   39: # FIX-305: Persistent cost ledger
   40: _COST_LEDGER_PATH = Path(os.getenv("PG_COST_LEDGER_PATH", "logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl"))
   41: 
   42: OPENROUTER_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "")
   43: OPENROUTER_BASE_URL = os.getenv(
   44:     "OPENROUTER_BASE_URL", "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
   45: )
   46: OPENROUTER_MODEL = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_DEFAULT_MODEL", "qwen/qwen3.5-plus-02-15")
   47: OPENROUTER_BUDGET_USD = float(os.getenv("OPENROUTER_BUDGET_USD", "50.0"))
   48: 
   49: # R-2 (readiness gate): hard per-run cost cap. A single honest-rebuild
   50: # run (scope+retrieval+generate+judge) should cost $0.005-$0.01. Cap at
   51: # $0.10 default so a runaway regen loop or recursive outline can't burn
   52: # hours of budget before we notice. Measured by cumulative cost across
   53: # ALL OpenRouterClient instances instantiated within a single run.
   54: PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN = float(os.getenv("PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN", "0.10"))
   55: 
   56: # BUG-B-201 fix (pass 2 remediation): per-task ambient state via
   57: # contextvars so concurrent async run_one_query() calls don't stomp
   58: # each other's run_id + cost accumulator.
   59: #
   60: # Pre-fix (commit f632ee5), `_CURRENT_RUN_ID` and `_RUN_COST_USD` were
   61: # module-level globals. Pass 2 showed that under `asyncio.gather`,
   62: # a later run_one_query overwrote the earlier run's ambient id before
   63: # downstream OpenRouterClient() construction — tagging the first run's
   64: # LLM calls with the second run's id.
   65: #
   66: # Post-fix: ContextVar scopes per asyncio Task. Each task sees its own
   67: # run_id + cost without threading explicit kwargs through the call graph.
   68: # Synchronous / serial callers still work because ContextVar defaults
   69: # to the module-default if no set_* has run in the current task.
   70: import contextvars
   71: 
   72: _RUN_COST_LOCK = __import__("threading").Lock()
   73: _RUN_COST_CTX: contextvars.ContextVar[float] = contextvars.ContextVar(
   74:     "_RUN_COST_USD", default=0.0,
   75: )
   76: _CURRENT_RUN_ID_CTX: contextvars.ContextVar[str | None] = contextvars.ContextVar(
   77:     "_CURRENT_RUN_ID", default=None,
   78: )
   79: 
   80: 
   81: def set_current_run_id(run_id: str | None) -> None:
   82:     """Set the ambient run-id for the current async task. Each
   83:     asyncio.Task has its own context, so concurrent runs don't stomp.
   84:     """
   85:     _CURRENT_RUN_ID_CTX.set(run_id)
   86: 
   87: 
   88: def current_run_id() -> str | None:
   89:     return _CURRENT_RUN_ID_CTX.get()
   90: 
   91: 
   92: class BudgetExceededError(RuntimeError):
   93:     """Raised when PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN is breached mid-run."""
   94: 
   95: 
   96: def reset_run_cost() -> None:
   97:     """Reset the current task's run-cost accumulator to 0. Call at the
   98:     start of a run. Uses ContextVar so concurrent async tasks each get
   99:     their own zero-start."""
  100:     _RUN_COST_CTX.set(0.0)
  101: 
  102: 
  103: def current_run_cost() -> float:
  104:     """Return the cumulative cost of the current run (USD), scoped to
  105:     the current asyncio task via ContextVar."""
  106:     return _RUN_COST_CTX.get()
  107: 
  108: 
  109: def _add_run_cost(delta: float) -> None:
  110:     # BUG-B-201: ContextVar-scoped cost accumulator. Each async task has
  111:     # its own run-cost; concurrent gather() of run_one_query() no longer
  112:     # stomps each other.
  113:     _RUN_COST_CTX.set(_RUN_COST_CTX.get() + float(delta))
  114: 
  115: 
  116: # Codex round 1 B-4: conservative per-model prices used when OpenRouter
  117: # omits usage.cost. Prices are $/M tokens and represent the UPPER END of
  118: # published rates for each provider family (if a cheaper provider is used
  119: # we overcharge the run cost, which is the safe direction for a budget
  120: # guard). When the model is unknown we use OPUS-tier defaults.
  121: _PRICE_TABLE_USD_PER_M: dict[str, tuple[float, float]] = {
  122:     # model prefix  :  (input $/M, output $/M)
  123:     # IMPORTANT: longer/more-specific prefixes first. Dict insertion order
  124:     # = iteration order = first-match-wins per _impute_cost_from_tokens.
  125:     "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro":   (0.435, 0.87),
  126:     "deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash": (0.14, 0.28),
  127:     "deepseek/":       (0.27, 0.38),
  128:     "qwen/qwen3-8b":   (0.05, 0.40),
  129:     "qwen/qwen3-32b":  (0.10, 0.60),
  130:     "qwen/":           (0.10, 0.60),
  131:     "z-ai/glm-5.1":    (0.60, 2.20),
  132:     "z-ai/":           (0.60, 2.20),
  133:     "meta-llama/":     (0.30, 0.90),
  134:     "google/gemma-4-31b-it": (0.13, 0.38),
  135:     "google/gemma":    (0.05, 0.30),
  136:     "google/gemini":   (1.25, 5.00),
  137:     "mistralai/":      (0.30, 0.90),
  138:     "moonshotai/":     (0.60, 2.50),
  139:     "openai/":         (3.00, 10.00),
  140:     "anthropic/":      (3.00, 15.00),
  141: }
  142: 
  143: _DEFAULT_PRICE_PER_M = (3.00, 15.00)  # Opus-tier worst-case
  144: 
  145: 
  146: def _impute_cost_from_tokens(
  147:     model: str,
  148:     input_tokens: int,
  149:     output_tokens: int,
  150:     reasoning_tokens: int,
  151: ) -> float:
  152:     """Estimate $/call from token counts + published prices. Reasoning
  153:     tokens bill at the output rate (matches OpenAI / Anthropic practice).
  154:     Used by the budget guard when OpenRouter omits usage.cost.
  155: 
  156:     Defensive: token counts are clamped to >=0. A corrupted API response
  157:     returning negative tokens must NOT produce a negative cost that
  158:     would silently shrink the accumulated run budget and let a runaway
  159:     loop keep calling past PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN.
  160:     """
  161:     # Clamp negative token counts — these can only come from a
  162:     # corrupted API response. A negative cost here would be silently
  163:     # absorbed by _add_run_cost and weaken the budget guard.
  164:     input_tokens = max(0, int(input_tokens))
  165:     output_tokens = max(0, int(output_tokens))
  166:     reasoning_tokens = max(0, int(reasoning_tokens))
  167:     if input_tokens == 0 and output_tokens == 0 and reasoning_tokens == 0:
  168:         return 0.0
  169:     model_lower = (model or "").lower()
  170:     input_rate, output_rate = _DEFAULT_PRICE_PER_M
  171:     for prefix, (in_r, out_r) in _PRICE_TABLE_USD_PER_M.items():
  172:         if model_lower.startswith(prefix):
  173:             input_rate, output_rate = in_r, out_r
  174:             break
  175:     return (
  176:         (input_tokens / 1_000_000.0) * input_rate
  177:         + ((output_tokens + reasoning_tokens) / 1_000_000.0) * output_rate
  178:     )
  179: 
  180: 
  181: def check_run_budget(anticipated_additional: float = 0.0) -> None:
  182:     """Raise BudgetExceededError if the next call would exceed the cap.
  183: 
  184:     anticipated_additional: optional estimate of the upcoming call cost
  185:     (used only for guard-before-call — the actual cost is recorded by
  186:     `_add_run_cost()` after the call returns).
  187:     """
  188:     current = current_run_cost()
  189:     projected = current + max(0.0, float(anticipated_additional))
  190:     if projected > PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN:
  191:         raise BudgetExceededError(
  192:             f"PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN={PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN:.4f} exceeded: "
  193:             f"current=${current:.4f} + anticipated=${anticipated_additional:.4f} "
  194:             f"= ${projected:.4f}. Set PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN higher or call "
  195:             f"reset_run_cost() between runs."
  196:         )
  197:

 1793:     async def generate(
 1794:         self,
 1795:         prompt: str,
 1796:         system: str = "",
 1797:         max_tokens: int = 4096,
 1798:         temperature: float = 0.7,
 1799:         timeout: Optional[float] = None,
 1800:         reasoning_max_tokens: Optional[int] = None,
 1801:         reasoning_exclude: Optional[bool] = None,
 1802:     ) -> LLMResponse:
 1803:         """
 1804:         Prose/output call — reasoning OFF, clean output only.
 1805: 
 1806:         Use for: section writing, report prose, summaries.
 1807: 
 1808:         No CoT in the output. No reasoning field. Just clean text.
 1809: 
 1810:         COT-2: If content is empty but reasoning has substance, try to
 1811:         extract the answer from reasoning (provider misroute). If that
 1812:         fails, retry once (OpenRouter may re-route to a different provider).
 1813:         """
 1814:         messages = []
 1815:         if system:
 1816:             messages.append({"role": "system", "content": system})
 1817:         messages.append({"role": "user", "content": prompt})
 1818: 
 1819:         result = await self._call(
 1820:             messages=messages,
 1821:             call_type="generate",
 1822:             reasoning_enabled=False,
 1823:             temperature=temperature,
 1824:             max_tokens=max_tokens,
 1825:             timeout=timeout or DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS,
 1826:             reasoning_max_tokens=reasoning_max_tokens,
 1827:             reasoning_exclude=reasoning_exclude,
 1828:         )
 1829: 
 1830:         # TWO-POOL: Content field = output. Reasoning field = logged separately.
 1831:         # For GLM-5, both pools are populated when max_tokens is sufficient.
 1832:         # Log reasoning for monitoring but NEVER merge into content.
 1833:         if result.reasoning and self.model in _ALWAYS_REASON_MODELS:
 1834:             logger.info(
 1835:                 "[polaris graph] POOL-1: Reasoning logged (%d chars) for generate()",
 1836:                 len(result.reasoning),
 1837:             )
 1838: 
 1839:         if result.content and result.content.strip():
 1840:             return result
 1841: 
 1842:         # Fallback: content empty (max_tokens too low or provider issue).
 1843:         # Try </think> extraction from reasoning as last resort.
 1844:         if result.reasoning:
 1845:             extracted = _extract_answer_from_reasoning(result.reasoning)
 1846:             if extracted:
 1847:                 logger.warning(
 1848:                     "[polaris graph] POOL-FALLBACK: generate() content empty — "
 1849:                     "recovered %d chars from reasoning via </think> split",
 1850:                     len(extracted),
 1851:                 )
 1852:                 result = LLMResponse(
 1853:                     content=extracted,
 1854:                     reasoning=result.reasoning,
 1855:                     input_tokens=result.input_tokens,
 1856:                     output_tokens=result.output_tokens,
 1857:                     reasoning_tokens=result.reasoning_tokens,
 1858:                     model=result.model,
 1859:                     duration_ms=result.duration_ms,
 1860:                     raw_response=result.raw_response,
 1861:                 )
 1862:             elif self.model in _ALWAYS_REASON_MODELS:
 1863:                 # FIX-GLM5: Models that always reason don't use </think> tags.
 1864:                 # Use reasoning directly as content (same as COT-3 free-form).
 1865:                 # FIX-GLM5-COT: Strip chain-of-thought markers from reasoning
 1866:                 # before using as content. GLM-5 puts "1. **Analyze the Request:**"
 1867:                 # and "The user wants..." thinking before the actual output.
 1868:                 _cleaned_reasoning = result.reasoning
 1869:                 import re as _re
 1870: 
 1871:                 # Strategy 1: Find end of numbered thinking steps pattern
 1872:                 # GLM-5 uses "1. **Analyze...**\n2. **Draft...**\n" then output
 1873:                 _thinking_block = _re.search(
 1874:                     r"\n(?=(?:##\s|[A-Z][a-z]{3,}.*(?:\[|\(95%|MD\s|SMD\s|HR\s|RR\s|OR\s)))",
 1875:                     _cleaned_reasoning,
 1876:                 )
 1877: 
 1878:                 # Strategy 2: Find after "Now let me" / "Here is" / "Output:" patterns
 1879:                 if not _thinking_block or _thinking_block.start() < 50:
 1880:                     _thinking_block = _re.search(
 1881:                         r"\n(?:(?:Now let me|Here is|Here's|Output:?|The revised|The edited|REVISED|EDITED).*\n)",
 1882:                         _cleaned_reasoning,
 1883:                         _re.IGNORECASE,
 1884:                     )
 1885:                     if _thinking_block:
 1886:                         # Use the end of the "Now let me" line as the start of real content
 1887:                         _tb_pos = _thinking_block.end()
 1888:                         class _PosHolder:
 1889:                             def start(self):
 1890:                                 return _tb_pos
 1891:                         _thinking_block = _PosHolder()
 1892: 
 1893:                 # Strategy 3: Original domain-keyword detection
 1894:                 if not _thinking_block or _thinking_block.start() < 50:
 1895:                     _thinking_block = _re.search(
 1896:                         r"(?:^|\n)(?=[A-Z][a-z].*(?:\[CITE:|\[\d+\]|fasting|study|meta|trial|evidence|research|protocol|clinical))",
 1897:                         _cleaned_reasoning,
 1898:                     )
 1899: 
 1900:                 if _thinking_block and _thinking_block.start() > 100:
 1901:                     _cleaned_reasoning = _cleaned_reasoning[_thinking_block.start():].lstrip()

 1903:                         "[polaris graph] FIX-GLM5-COT: Stripped %d chars CoT prefix "
 1904:                         "from generate() reasoning",

 1906:                     )

 1911:                 )
 1912:                 result = LLMResponse(
 1913:                     content=_cleaned_reasoning,

 1916:                     output_tokens=result.output_tokens,

 1919:                     duration_ms=result.duration_ms,
 1920:                     raw_response=result.raw_response,
 1921:                 )

 1924:                 # reasoning-first models (e.g. DeepSeek V4 Pro) that route

 1935:                     "reasoning-first — using reasoning as content directly.",

 1937:                 )

 1939:                     content=result.reasoning,

 1947:                 )
 1948:             else:
 1949:                 # Reasoning is too sparse to be a real answer — retry once

 1955:                 )

   76: async def test_content_present_wins_over_reasoning() -> None:

   84:     response_with_both = _make_response(
   85:         content="Final answer: 42",
   86:         reasoning="Let me think about this... I considered options A, B, C...",
   87:     )
   88: 
   89:     with patch.object(client, "_call", new=AsyncMock(return_value=response_with_both)):
   90:         result = await client.generate(prompt="What is the answer?")
   91: 
   92:     assert result.content == "Final answer: 42"
   93:     assert "Let me think" in (result.reasoning or "")
   94: 
   95:     await client.close()

   98: @pytest.mark.asyncio
   99: async def test_think_tag_extraction_still_wins() -> None:
  100:     """Regression: </think> extraction continues to fire when applicable.
  101: 
  102:     Models that emit `<think>cot</think>answer` into reasoning_content should
  103:     still go through the </think> extractor, NOT the response-shape-centric
  104:     fall-through. This preserves the cleanest path.
  105:     """
  106:     client = OpenRouterClient(model="some-think-tag-model")
  107: 
  108:     cot_with_tag = (

  111:     )
  112:     response_with_tag = _make_response(content="", reasoning=cot_with_tag)
  113:

  115:         result = await client.generate(prompt="Summarize")

  153: @pytest.mark.asyncio
  154: async def test_sparse_reasoning_falls_to_retry_path() -> None:
  155:     """Edge: reasoning under 100 chars is too sparse to be a real answer.
  156: 
  157:     Below threshold the call falls into the COT-2 retry path (which is the
  158:     correct behavior — empty reasoning indicates a transient provider issue,
  159:     not a reasoning-first model emitting its full answer there).
  160:     """
  161:     client = OpenRouterClient(model="deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro")
  162: 
  163:     sparse = _make_response(content="", reasoning="ok")
  164:     full_after_retry = _make_response(content="The actual answer here.", reasoning="")
  165: 
  166:     call_mock = AsyncMock(side_effect=[sparse, full_after_retry])
  167:     with patch.object(client, "_call", new=call_mock):
  168:         result = await client.generate(prompt="What?")
  169: 
  170:     assert call_mock.await_count == 2, "Sparse reasoning must trigger retry"
  171:     assert result.content == "The actual answer here."
  172: 
  173:     await client.close()
  174:

 2008:     async def generate_structured(
 2009:         self,
 2010:         prompt: str,
 2011:         schema: Type[T],
 2012:         system: str = "",
 2013:         max_tokens: int = 8192,
 2014:         timeout: Optional[float] = None,
 2015:         reasoning_enabled: bool = False,
 2016:         reasoning_effort: str = "high",
 2017:         reasoning_max_tokens: Optional[int] = None,
 2018:         reasoning_exclude: Optional[bool] = None,
 2019:     ) -> T:
 2020:         """
 2021:         Structured output call — reasoning OFF by default, returns parsed schema.
 2022: 
 2023:         When reasoning is DISABLED and PG_STRICT_JSON_SCHEMA=1 (default),
 2024:         sends response_format with json_schema + strict:true to enforce
 2025:         schema compliance at the provider level (Qwen 3.5 Plus supports this).
 2026: 
 2027:         When reasoning is ENABLED, relies on prompt-based JSON extraction
 2028:         because thinking + json_schema response_format are incompatible.
 2029: 
 2030:         FIX-QM1: When reasoning IS explicitly enabled, checks both content
 2031:         and reasoning_content for JSON (some models put JSON in reasoning).
 2032:         """
 2033:         # Use strict:true JSON schema when reasoning is disabled and env allows.
 2034:         # Qwen3.5-Plus supports strict schema enforcement. Gate behind
 2035:         # PG_STRICT_JSON_SCHEMA (default "1") for backwards compatibility.
 2036:         # When reasoning is enabled, skip — thinking + json_schema are incompatible.
 2037:         #
 2038:         # FIX-GLM5-STRUCTURED (2026-04-12): For models in _ALWAYS_REASON_MODELS,
 2039:         # reasoning is forced ON by _call() regardless of this parameter. Without
 2040:         # this check, GLM 5.1 receives BOTH response_format=json_schema strict:true
 2041:         # AND reasoning=enabled, which causes complex-schema calls (StormOutlinePlan,
 2042:         # SourceAnalysisBatch, ReportOutline) to dump 17K+ chars of prose into
 2043:         # reasoning_content with content="" (empty). Symptom in PG_TEST_090 trace:
 2044:         # "Content empty for structured:StormOutlinePlan, reasoning has 17958 chars".
 2045:         # The fix makes generate_structured aware that these models always reason,
 2046:         # so it skips response_format and uses prompt-based JSON extraction (which
 2047:         # works because the prompt's json_hint instructs JSON-only output).
 2048:         response_format = None
 2049:         strict_schema_enabled = os.getenv("PG_STRICT_JSON_SCHEMA", "1") == "1"
 2050:         _effective_reasoning = reasoning_enabled or (self.model in _ALWAYS_REASON_MODELS)
 2051:         if not _effective_reasoning and strict_schema_enabled:
 2052:             try:
 2053:                 json_schema = schema.model_json_schema()
 2054:                 response_format = {
 2055:                     "type": "json_schema",
 2056:                     "json_schema": {
 2057:                         "name": schema.__name__,
 2058:                         "strict": True,
 2059:                         "schema": json_schema,
 2060:                     },
 2061:                 }
 2062:             except Exception as schema_exc:

 2065:                     "— falling back to prompt-based JSON",

 1039: 
 1040:     async def _call(
 1041:         self,
 1042:         messages: list[dict[str, str]],
 1043:         call_type: str,
 1044:         reasoning_enabled: bool = True,
 1045:         reasoning_effort: str = "high",
 1046:         temperature: float = 0.7,
 1047:         max_tokens: int = 16384,
 1048:         response_format: Optional[dict] = None,
 1049:         timeout: Optional[float] = None,
 1050:         reasoning_max_tokens: Optional[int] = None,
 1051:         reasoning_exclude: Optional[bool] = None,
 1052:     ) -> LLMResponse:
 1053:         """Make a single API call to OpenRouter.
 1054: 
 1055:         A8.2: Acquires global concurrency semaphore before executing.
 1056:         This prevents GPU OOM on sovereign deployments and 429 rate
 1057:         limits on cloud APIs by limiting parallel LLM calls.
 1058:         """
 1059:         # A8.2: Global concurrency control
 1060:         from src.providers.llm_provider import get_semaphore
 1061:         semaphore = get_semaphore()
 1062:         async with semaphore:
 1063:             return await self._call_impl(
 1064:                 messages, call_type, reasoning_enabled, reasoning_effort,
 1065:                 temperature, max_tokens, response_format, timeout,
 1066:                 reasoning_max_tokens, reasoning_exclude,
 1067:             )
 1068: 
 1069:     async def _call_impl(
 1070:         self,
 1071:         messages: list[dict[str, str]],
 1072:         call_type: str,
 1073:         reasoning_enabled: bool = True,
 1074:         reasoning_effort: str = "high",
 1075:         temperature: float = 0.7,
 1076:         max_tokens: int = 16384,
 1077:         response_format: Optional[dict] = None,
 1078:         timeout: Optional[float] = None,
 1079:         reasoning_max_tokens: Optional[int] = None,
 1080:         reasoning_exclude: Optional[bool] = None,
 1081:     ) -> LLMResponse:
 1082:         """Internal implementation — called within semaphore context."""
 1083:         # FIX-402: Short-circuit all calls once 402 Payment Required is received.
 1084:         if OpenRouterClient._billing_exhausted:
 1085:             raise BillingExhaustedException(
 1086:                 "OpenRouter billing exhausted (402 circuit breaker OPEN). "
 1087:                 "No API calls will be attempted."
 1088:             )
 1089: 
 1090:         if self.usage.budget_exhausted:
 1091:             raise BudgetExhaustedError(
 1092:                 f"Budget exhausted: ${self.usage.total_cost_usd:.2f} "
 1093:                 f">= ${self.usage.budget_usd:.2f}"
 1094:             )
 1095: 
 1096:         # R-2: check the shared per-run budget cap. Each client
 1097:         # contributes to _RUN_COST_USD after each call; here we abort
 1098:         # early if the cap is already breached rather than fire another
 1099:         # call and discover the overage after the fact.
 1100:         check_run_budget(anticipated_additional=0.0)
 1101: 
 1102:         # K2-3: Filter reasoning_content: null from messages to prevent
 1103:         # prompt pollution (literal "null" causes model confusion).
 1104:         sanitized_messages = []
 1105:         for msg in messages:
 1106:             clean_msg = {k: v for k, v in msg.items() if v is not None}
 1107:             sanitized_messages.append(clean_msg)
 1108: 
 1109:         body: dict[str, Any] = {
 1110:             "model": self.model,
 1111:             "messages": sanitized_messages,
 1112:             "temperature": temperature,
 1113:             "max_tokens": max_tokens,
 1114:             "stream": True,
 1115:         }
 1116: 
 1117:         # GLM-5 TWO-POOL ARCHITECTURE:
 1118:         # Pool 1 (reasoning field): reasoning tokens → logged for monitoring
 1119:         # Pool 2 (content field): actual output → used for report
 1120:         # GLM-5 returns BOTH when max_tokens is sufficient (tested:
 1121:         #   max_tokens=2000 → 1313 chars content + 5868 chars reasoning).
 1122:         # CRITICAL: Never merge reasoning into content. Never disable reasoning.
 1123:         # Reasoning drives analytical quality (SO WHAT, contradictions, GRADE).
 1124:         if self.model in _ALWAYS_REASON_MODELS:
 1125:             # W1.2 merge-not-replace: defaults fill only keys the caller didn't set.
 1126:             # Caller-passed reasoning_max_tokens / reasoning_exclude now reach the API.
 1127:             reasoning_dict: dict[str, Any] = {"exclude": False}
 1128:             if reasoning_exclude is not None:
 1129:                 reasoning_dict["exclude"] = reasoning_exclude
 1130:             if reasoning_max_tokens is not None:
 1131:                 # OpenRouter constraint: effort and max_tokens are mutually exclusive.
 1132:                 reasoning_dict["max_tokens"] = reasoning_max_tokens
 1133:             else:
 1134:                 reasoning_dict["effort"] = reasoning_effort or "high"
 1135:             body["reasoning"] = reasoning_dict
 1136:             body["temperature"] = 1.0  # GLM-5 docs: temp 1.0 for thinking
 1137:             # Enforce minimum max_tokens so reasoning doesn't starve content.
 1138:             # At max_tokens<2000, GLM-5 spends entire budget on reasoning,
 1139:             # leaving content empty. Tested: 2000 → 1313 chars content.
 1140:             _min_tokens = int(os.getenv("PG_GLM5_MIN_MAX_TOKENS", "4096"))
 1141:             if body.get("max_tokens", 0) < _min_tokens:
 1142:                 body["max_tokens"] = _min_tokens
 1143:         elif reasoning_enabled:
 1144:             reasoning_dict = {"effort": reasoning_effort, "enabled": True}
 1145:             if reasoning_max_tokens is not None:
 1146:                 reasoning_dict.pop("effort", None)
 1147:                 reasoning_dict["max_tokens"] = reasoning_max_tokens
 1148:             if reasoning_exclude is not None:
 1149:                 reasoning_dict["exclude"] = reasoning_exclude
 1150:             body["reasoning"] = reasoning_dict
 1151: 
 1152:         if response_format:
 1153:             body["response_format"] = response_format
 1154:             # FIX-QWEN-2: Use non-streaming for json_object responses.
 1155:             # Alibaba provider (Qwen 3.5 Plus) returns 502 on json_object+stream.
 1156:             if response_format.get("type") == "json_object" and not reasoning_enabled:
 1157:                 body["stream"] = False
 1158: 
 1159:         # Provider routing from env; empty = let OpenRouter auto-route
 1160:         provider_order_str = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_PROVIDER_ORDER", "")
 1161:         provider_order = [p.strip() for p in provider_order_str.split(",") if p.strip()]
 1162:         allow_fb = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_ALLOW_FALLBACKS", "true").lower() == "true"
 1163:         require_params = os.getenv("OPENROUTER_REQUIRE_PARAMETERS", "true").lower() == "true"
 1164:         provider_block: dict[str, Any] = {
 1165:             "allow_fallbacks": allow_fb,
 1166:             "require_parameters": require_params,
 1167:         }
 1168:         if provider_order:
 1169:             provider_block["order"] = provider_order
 1170:         body["provider"] = provider_block
 1171: 
 1172:         start = time.monotonic()
 1173:         last_error = None
 1174: 
 1175:         for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES + 1):
 1176:             try:
 1177:                 actual_timeout = timeout or DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS
 1178: 
 1179:                 if body.get("stream", True):
 1180:                     # Streaming path — accumulate SSE chunks
 1181:                     content_text, reasoning_text, stream_usage = await asyncio.wait_for(
 1182:                         self._read_stream(body, actual_timeout),
 1183:                         timeout=actual_timeout + 30,
 1184:                     )
 1185:                     data = {
 1186:                         "choices": [{
 1187:                             "message": {
 1188:                                 "content": content_text,
 1189:                                 "reasoning_content": reasoning_text,
 1190:                             },
 1191:                             "finish_reason": "stop",
 1192:                         }],
 1193:                         "usage": stream_usage,
 1194:                         "model": self.model,
 1195:                     }
 1196:                 else:
 1197:                     # FIX-QWEN-2: Non-streaming path for json_object
 1198:                     resp = await asyncio.wait_for(
 1199:                         self._client.post(
 1200:                             "/chat/completions",
 1201:                             json=body,
 1202:                             timeout=httpx.Timeout(actual_timeout, connect=30.0),
 1203:                         ),
 1204:                         timeout=actual_timeout + 30,
 1205:                     )
 1206:                     resp.raise_for_status()
 1207:                     data = resp.json()
 1208:                     # FIX-QWEN-2: Detect provider errors in non-streaming response
 1209:                     if data.get("error") and not data.get("choices"):
 1210:                         err = data["error"]
 1211:                         raise RuntimeError(
 1212:                             f"Provider error (code={err.get('code', '?')}): "
 1213:                             f"{err.get('message', str(err))[:200]}"
 1214:                         )
 1215:                 break
 1216: 
 1217:             except asyncio.TimeoutError:
 1218:                 logger.warning(
 1219:                     "[polaris graph] FIX-SCHEMA-5: asyncio timeout after %ds "
 1220:                     "for %s (attempt %d/%d)",
 1221:                     actual_timeout + 30, call_type, attempt + 1, MAX_RETRIES + 1,
 1222:                 )
 1223:                 last_error = TimeoutError(
 1224:                     f"asyncio timeout after {actual_timeout + 30}s"
 1225:                 )
 1226:                 if attempt < MAX_RETRIES:
 1227:                     await asyncio.sleep(RETRY_BACKOFF_BASE ** attempt)
 1228:                     continue
 1229:                 raise TimeoutError(
 1230:                     f"All {MAX_RETRIES + 1} attempts timed out for {call_type}"
 1231:                 ) from None
 1232: 
 1233:             except httpx.HTTPStatusError as exc:
 1234:                 last_error = exc
 1235:                 status = exc.response.status_code
 1236: 
 1237:                 # FIX-402: Payment Required — set circuit breaker and stop immediately.
 1238:                 # Prevents hundreds of wasted retries against a billing-blocked account.
 1239:                 if status == 402:
 1240:                     OpenRouterClient._billing_exhausted = True
 1241:                     self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1242:                     logger.critical(
 1243:                         "[polaris graph] FIX-402: OpenRouter returned 402 Payment Required. "
 1244:                         "Circuit breaker OPEN — all subsequent LLM calls will be rejected. "
 1245:                         "Check billing at https://openrouter.ai/settings/credits"
 1246:                     )
 1247:                     raise BillingExhaustedException(
 1248:                         "OpenRouter 402 Payment Required — billing exhausted. "
 1249:                         "No further API calls will be attempted."
 1250:                     ) from exc
 1251: 
 1252:                 # Rate limit — back off
 1253:                 if status == 429:
 1254:                     wait = RETRY_BACKOFF_BASE ** (attempt + 1)
 1255:                     logger.warning(
 1256:                         "[polaris graph] Rate limited, waiting %.1fs (attempt %d/%d)",
 1257:                         wait, attempt + 1, MAX_RETRIES + 1,
 1258:                     )
 1259:                     await asyncio.sleep(wait)
 1260:                     continue
 1261: 
 1262:                 # Server error — retry
 1263:                 if status >= 500:
 1264:                     wait = RETRY_BACKOFF_BASE ** attempt
 1265:                     logger.warning(
 1266:                         "[polaris graph] Server error %d, retrying in %.1fs",
 1267:                         status, wait,
 1268:                     )
 1269:                     await asyncio.sleep(wait)
 1270:                     continue
 1271: 
 1272:                 # Client error — don't retry
 1273:                 self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1274:                 raise
 1275: 
 1276:             except RuntimeError as exc:
 1277:                 # FIX-QWEN-1: SSE mid-stream/provider errors — retry
 1278:                 last_error = exc
 1279:                 if attempt < MAX_RETRIES:
 1280:                     wait = RETRY_BACKOFF_BASE ** (attempt + 1)
 1281:                     logger.warning(
 1282:                         "[polaris graph] FIX-QWEN-1: SSE error, retrying in %.1fs "
 1283:                         "(attempt %d/%d): %s",
 1284:                         wait, attempt + 1, MAX_RETRIES + 1,
 1285:                         str(exc)[:100],
 1286:                     )
 1287:                     await asyncio.sleep(wait)
 1288:                     continue
 1289:                 self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1290:                 raise
 1291: 
 1292:             except (httpx.TimeoutException, httpx.ConnectError) as exc:
 1293:                 last_error = exc
 1294:                 # FIX-052B: DNS failures get longer backoff (outages last 10-60s)
 1295:                 is_dns_failure = "getaddrinfo" in str(exc).lower()
 1296:                 if attempt < MAX_RETRIES:
 1297:                     if is_dns_failure:
 1298:                         dns_wait = float(os.getenv("PG_DNS_RETRY_BACKOFF", "30"))
 1299:                         logger.warning(
 1300:                             "[polaris graph] DNS failure (getaddrinfo), "
 1301:                             "waiting %.0fs before retry (attempt %d/%d): %s",
 1302:                             dns_wait, attempt + 1, MAX_RETRIES + 1,
 1303:                             str(exc)[:100],
 1304:                         )
 1305:                         await asyncio.sleep(dns_wait)
 1306:                     else:
 1307:                         wait = RETRY_BACKOFF_BASE ** (attempt + 1)
 1308:                         logger.warning(
 1309:                             "[polaris graph] Connection error, retrying in %.1fs: %s",
 1310:                             wait, str(exc)[:100],
 1311:                         )
 1312:                         await asyncio.sleep(wait)
 1313:                     continue
 1314:                 self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1315:                 raise
 1316:         else:
 1317:             self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1318:             raise last_error  # type: ignore[misc]
 1319: 
 1320:         duration_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
 1321: 
 1322:         # SF-16: Check for empty choices before indexing
 1323:         choices = data.get("choices", [])
 1324:         if not choices:
 1325:             self.usage.total_errors += 1
 1326:             raise ValueError(
 1327:                 f"API returned no choices in response for {call_type} "
 1328:                 f"(model={data.get('model', '?')})"
 1329:             )
 1330:         choice = choices[0]
 1331:         message = choice.get("message", {})
 1332:         content = message.get("content", "")
 1333: 
 1334:         # Extract reasoning — OpenRouter returns it in reasoning_content
 1335:         # or in reasoning_details array depending on provider
 1336:         reasoning = None
 1337:         reasoning_tokens = 0
 1338: 
 1339:         if "reasoning_content" in message and message["reasoning_content"]:
 1340:             reasoning = message["reasoning_content"]
 1341:         elif "reasoning" in message and message["reasoning"]:
 1342:             # Some providers return it as 'reasoning'
 1343:             reasoning = message["reasoning"]
 1344: 
 1345:         # SF-30: Token usage from response — warn if missing
 1346:         usage_data = data.get("usage", {})
 1347:         if not usage_data:
 1348:             # FIX-O2: Estimate tokens from content length instead of defaulting to 0
 1349:             est_input = sum(len(m.get("content", "")) for m in messages) // 4
 1350:             est_output = len(content) // 4 if content else 0
 1351:             logger.warning(
 1352:                 "[polaris graph] FIX-O2: API response missing usage data for %s "
 1353:                 "— estimating %d/%d tokens from content length",
 1354:                 call_type, est_input, est_output,
 1355:             )
 1356:             usage_data = {
 1357:                 "prompt_tokens": est_input,
 1358:                 "completion_tokens": est_output,
 1359:             }
 1360:         input_tokens = usage_data.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
 1361:         output_tokens = usage_data.get("completion_tokens", 0)
 1362:         reasoning_tokens = usage_data.get("reasoning_tokens", 0)
 1363:         # FIX-QM11b: OpenRouter nests reasoning_tokens inside
 1364:         # completion_tokens_details, not at top level of usage
 1365:         if not reasoning_tokens:
 1366:             ctd = usage_data.get("completion_tokens_details", {})
 1367:             if ctd:
 1368:                 reasoning_tokens = ctd.get("reasoning_tokens", 0)
 1369: 
 1370:         # FIX-C2: Extract API-reported cost for accurate billing.
 1371:         # Codex round 1 B-4: OpenRouter omits `usage.cost` for some models,
 1372:         # which let the run budget record $0.00 even when tokens were
 1373:         # consumed. Now: if cost is missing AND tokens were consumed,
 1374:         # impute a conservative upper bound from published per-model rates.
 1375:         api_cost = usage_data.get("cost", None)
 1376:         if api_cost is None or api_cost == 0:
 1377:             # Use per-model price table; defaults reflect observed upper-
 1378:             # bound ($10/M output for opus-tier models) so the guard is
 1379:             # always conservative. Token totals may be 0 if the response
 1380:             # carried no usage block at all.
 1381:             imputed = _impute_cost_from_tokens(
 1382:                 self.model, input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_tokens,
 1383:             )
 1384:             if api_cost is None and imputed > 0:
 1385:                 logger.warning(
 1386:                     "[polaris graph] B-4: OpenRouter omitted usage.cost "
 1387:                     "for model=%r; imputed cost=$%.6f from tokens "
 1388:                     "(in=%d out=%d reasoning=%d). Budget guard uses the "
 1389:                     "imputed value so a missing cost field cannot bypass "
 1390:                     "PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN.",
 1391:                     self.model, imputed, input_tokens, output_tokens,
 1392:                     reasoning_tokens,
 1393:                 )
 1394:             api_cost = max(api_cost or 0.0, imputed)
 1395: 
 1396:         # Track usage
 1397:         self.usage.record(
 1398:             call_type=call_type,
 1399:             input_tokens=input_tokens,
 1400:             output_tokens=output_tokens,
 1401:             reasoning_tokens=reasoning_tokens,
 1402:             duration_ms=duration_ms,
 1403:             api_cost=api_cost,
 1404:         )
 1405:         # R-2: contribute to the shared run-cost counter AFTER the call.
 1406:         # Checking the cap BEFORE is done by callers via check_run_budget().
 1407:         _add_run_cost(api_cost)
 1408: 
 1409:         # COT-1: Strict separation — NEVER mix reasoning into content.
 1410:         # The API separates content and reasoning_content correctly.
 1411:         # Callers (generate(), reason(), generate_structured()) handle
 1412:         # empty content at their own level with retry + extraction.
 1413:         # FIX-H2: Explicit warning and raise when BOTH content and reasoning are empty.
 1414:         # This catches degenerate API responses (0-token completions) instead of
 1415:         # silently returning empty strings that cascade into downstream failures.
 1416:         if not content and not reasoning:
 1417:             logger.warning(
 1418:                 "[polaris graph] FIX-H2: LLM returned empty content AND empty "
 1419:                 "reasoning_content for %s (model=%s, %d input tokens)",
 1420:                 call_type,
 1421:                 data.get("model", self.model),
 1422:                 input_tokens,
 1423:             )
 1424:             raise ValueError(
 1425:                 f"LLM returned no usable content for {call_type} "
 1426:                 f"(model={data.get('model', '?')}, input_tokens={input_tokens})"
 1427:             )
 1428: 
 1429:         if not content and reasoning:
 1430:             logger.warning(
 1431:                 "[polaris graph] COT-1: Content empty for %s, reasoning has "
 1432:                 "%d chars (reasoning_enabled=%s, response_format=%s). "
 1433:                 "Returning as-is — caller handles recovery.",
 1434:                 call_type,
 1435:                 len(reasoning),
 1436:                 reasoning_enabled,
 1437:                 bool(response_format),
 1438:             )
 1439: 
 1440:         result = LLMResponse(
 1441:             content=content,
 1442:             reasoning=reasoning,
 1443:             input_tokens=input_tokens,
 1444:             output_tokens=output_tokens,
 1445:             reasoning_tokens=reasoning_tokens,
 1446:             model=data.get("model", self.model),
 1447:             duration_ms=duration_ms,
 1448:             raw_response=data,
 1449:         )
 1450: 
 1451:         logger.info(
 1452:             "[polaris graph] %s completed: %d in/%d out/%d reasoning tokens, "
 1453:             "%.1fs, $%.4f cumulative",
 1454:             call_type,
 1455:             input_tokens,
 1456:             output_tokens,
 1457:             reasoning_tokens,
 1458:             duration_ms / 1000,
 1459:             self.usage.total_cost_usd,
 1460:         )
 1461: 
 1462:         # OBS-COST: Emit authoritative llm_call trace with real tokens + cost.
 1463:         # This is the SINGLE source of truth — agents no longer need to pass
 1464:         # tokens to tracer.llm_call() since the client does it here.
 1465:         _cost_tracer = _current_tracer.get(None)
 1466:         if _cost_tracer is not None:
 1467:             _node = call_type.split(":")[0] if ":" in call_type else "llm"
 1468:             _cost_tracer.llm_call(
 1469:                 node=_node,
 1470:                 call_type=call_type,
 1471:                 input_tokens=input_tokens,
 1472:                 output_tokens=output_tokens,
 1473:                 duration_ms=duration_ms,
 1474:                 reasoning_tokens=reasoning_tokens,
 1475:                 cost_usd=round(

  334:             f"evaluator is the primary mitigation for evaluator-generator "

  336:             f"arXiv:2601.22984)."
  337:         )

  341:             f"not match any known family prefix. Set PG_EVALUATOR_FAMILY_OVERRIDE."

  367: })

  369: # Pricing per million tokens (configurable per LAW VI)
  370: # Qwen 3.5 Plus: $0.26 input, $1.56 output
  371: # Use API-reported cost when available (FIX-C1)
  372: INPUT_COST_PER_M = float(os.getenv("OPENROUTER_INPUT_COST_PER_M", "0.26"))
  373: OUTPUT_COST_PER_M = float(os.getenv("OPENROUTER_OUTPUT_COST_PER_M", "1.56"))
  374: 
  375: # Timeouts — FIX-SCHEMA-5: reduced from 180/300 to 90/180.
  376: # Qwen 3.5 Plus typically responds in 10-60s. 3+ min means the API is hung.
  377: # asyncio.wait_for adds +30s grace period on top of these.
  378: DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = int(os.getenv("PG_LLM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "90"))
  379: LONG_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = int(os.getenv("PG_LLM_LONG_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "180"))

  385: 
  386: @dataclass
  387: class UsageTracker:
  388:     """Track token usage and cost across the session."""
  389: 
  390:     total_input_tokens: int = 0
  391:     total_output_tokens: int = 0
  392:     total_reasoning_tokens: int = 0
  393:     total_calls: int = 0
  394:     total_errors: int = 0
  395:     budget_usd: float = OPENROUTER_BUDGET_USD
  396:     session_id: str = ""  # FIX-F2: Distinguish runs in persistent cost ledger
  397:     _call_log: list = field(default_factory=list)
  398: 
  399:     # FIX-C2: Track API-reported cost for accurate billing
  400:     total_api_reported_cost: float = 0.0
  401: 
  402:     @property
  403:     def total_cost_usd(self) -> float:
  404:         # Prefer API-reported cost if available (accurate per-provider pricing)
  405:         if self.total_api_reported_cost > 0:
  406:             return self.total_api_reported_cost
  407:         input_cost = (self.total_input_tokens / 1_000_000) * INPUT_COST_PER_M
  408:         output_cost = (self.total_output_tokens / 1_000_000) * OUTPUT_COST_PER_M
  409:         return input_cost + output_cost
  410: 
  411:     @property
  412:     def budget_remaining_usd(self) -> float:
  413:         return self.budget_usd - self.total_cost_usd
  414: 
  415:     @property
  416:     def budget_exhausted(self) -> bool:
  417:         return self.total_cost_usd >= self.budget_usd
  418: 
  419:     def record(
  420:         self,
  421:         call_type: str,
  422:         input_tokens: int,
  423:         output_tokens: int,
  424:         reasoning_tokens: int = 0,
  425:         duration_ms: float = 0,
  426:         api_cost: float = 0.0,
  427:         prompt_component_tokens: dict | None = None,
  428:     ):
  429:         self.total_input_tokens += input_tokens
  430:         self.total_output_tokens += output_tokens
  431:         self.total_reasoning_tokens += reasoning_tokens
  432:         self.total_calls += 1
  433:         # FIX-C2: Accumulate API-reported cost for accurate billing
  434:         if api_cost > 0:
  435:             self.total_api_reported_cost += api_cost
  436:         call_cost = api_cost if api_cost > 0 else round(
  437:             (input_tokens / 1_000_000) * INPUT_COST_PER_M
  438:             + (output_tokens / 1_000_000) * OUTPUT_COST_PER_M,
  439:             6,
  440:         )
  441:         entry = {
  442:             "type": call_type,
  443:             "input_tokens": input_tokens,
  444:             "output_tokens": output_tokens,
  445:             "reasoning_tokens": reasoning_tokens,
  446:             "duration_ms": duration_ms,
  447:             "cost_usd": round(call_cost, 6),
  448:             "cumulative_cost_usd": round(self.total_cost_usd, 4),
  449:         }
  450:         # TIER-3 Stage 6: Optional prompt component breakdown
  451:         if prompt_component_tokens:
  452:             entry["prompt_components"] = prompt_component_tokens
  453:         self._call_log.append(entry)
  454: 
  455:         # FIX-305: Persist to JSONL cost ledger
  456:         self._append_ledger({
  457:             "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
  458:             "session_id": self.session_id,  # FIX-F2: Tag entries with run ID
  459:             "call_type": call_type,
  460:             "input_tokens": input_tokens,
  461:             "output_tokens": output_tokens,
  462:             "reasoning_tokens": reasoning_tokens,
  463:             "duration_ms": round(duration_ms, 1),
  464:             "cost_usd": round(call_cost, 6),
  465:             "cumulative_cost_usd": round(self.total_cost_usd, 4),
  466:         })
  467: 
  468:     def _append_ledger(self, entry: dict):
  469:         """Append a cost entry to the persistent JSONL ledger."""
  470:         try:
  471:             _COST_LEDGER_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
  472:             with open(_COST_LEDGER_PATH, "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
  473:                 f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
  474:         except Exception as exc:
  475:             # FIX-O1: Elevated from debug to warning for visibility
  476:             logger.warning("FIX-O1: Cost ledger write failed: %s", exc)
  477: 
  478:     def summary(self) -> dict:
  479:         return {
  480:             "total_calls": self.total_calls,
  481:             "total_errors": self.total_errors,
  482:             "total_input_tokens": self.total_input_tokens,
  483:             "total_output_tokens": self.total_output_tokens,
  484:             "total_reasoning_tokens": self.total_reasoning_tokens,
  485:             "total_cost_usd": round(self.total_cost_usd, 4),
  486:             "budget_remaining_usd": round(self.budget_remaining_usd, 4),
  487:         }
  488: 
  489: 
  490: @dataclass
  491: class LLMResponse:
  492:     """Structured response from an LLM call."""
  493: 
  494:     content: str
  495:     reasoning: Optional[str] = None
  496:     input_tokens: int = 0
  497:     output_tokens: int = 0
  498:     reasoning_tokens: int = 0
  499:     model: str = ""
  500:     duration_ms: float = 0
  501:     raw_response: Optional[dict] = None
  502: 
  503: 
  504: class BudgetExhaustedError(Exception):
  505:     """Raised when the OpenRouter budget is exhausted."""
  506: 
  507: 
  508: class BillingExhaustedException(Exception):
  509:     """Raised when OpenRouter returns 402 Payment Required.
  510: 
  511:     Once triggered, all subsequent calls are short-circuited to prevent
  512:     hundreds of wasted retries against a billing-blocked account.
  513:     """
  514: 
  515: 
  516: def _clean_json(raw: str) -> str:
  517:     """Clean LLM JSON output — strip code fences, fix control chars in strings."""
  518:     text = raw.strip()
  519:     # Strip markdown code fences
  520:     if text.startswith("```"):
  521:         # Remove opening fence (with optional language tag)
  522:         text = re.sub(r"^```(?:json)?\s*\n?", "", text)
  523:         # Remove closing fence
  524:         text = re.sub(r"\n?```\s*$", "", text)
  525:     # Remove non-printable control characters (not \n, \r, \t)
  526:     text = re.sub(r"[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f]", "", text)
  527:     # FIX-SCHEMA-6: Repair spurious quote before array values (legacy Kimi issue, kept for safety).
  528:     # Two patterns observed:
  529:     #   Pattern 1: {"analyses":":[{"source_url"...  (no space, regex works)
  530:     #   Pattern 2: {"analyses": ":[{","source_url"... (valid JSON, leave alone)
  531:     # Strategy: apply regex, verify JSON still parses, revert if broken.
  532:     # Pattern 2 is valid JSON (analyses=string, source_url=sibling key) and
  533:     # FIX-SCHEMA-7 in Pydantic validators recovers from it.
  534:     _before_schema6 = text
  535:     text = re.sub(r'"\s*:\s*":\s*\[', '": [', text)
  536:     if text != _before_schema6:
  537:         try:
  538:             json.loads(text)
  539:         except (ValueError, json.JSONDecodeError):
  540:             # Regex broke valid JSON (Pattern 2) — revert
  541:             text = _before_schema6
  542:     # Escape raw control chars INSIDE JSON strings (newlines, tabs, etc.)
  543:     # Without this, json.loads() rejects "analysis":"\n\nThe user..."
  544:     text = _escape_control_chars_in_strings(text)
  545:     return text.strip()
  546: 
  547: 
  548: def _extract_answer_from_reasoning(reasoning: str) -> Optional[str]:
  549:     """COT-4: Extract the actual answer from reasoning that contains both CoT and answer.
  550: 
  551:     Some providers put BOTH chain-of-thought AND the final answer into the
  552:     reasoning_content field, leaving content empty. The answer typically
  553:     appears after a </think> tag. This function splits on that tag and
  554:     returns the text after it (the actual answer).
  555: 
  556:     Returns:
  557:         The answer text after </think>, or None if no tag found or
  558:         only CoT present (no substantial text after tag).
  559:     """
  560:     if not reasoning:
  561:         return None
  562: 
  563:     # Split on </think> tag (case-insensitive)
  564:     parts = re.split(r"</think>", reasoning, maxsplit=1, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
  565:     if len(parts) < 2:
  566:         return None
  567: 
  568:     answer = parts[1].strip()
  569:     # Only return if there's substantial content after the tag
  570:     # (not just a few trailing chars or whitespace)
  571:     if len(answer) < 20:
  572:         return None
  573: 
  574:     return answer
  575:

 1517:                 "response_format": str(body.get("response_format", "")),
 1518:                 "prompt_messages": [
 1519:                     {"role": m.get("role", ""), "content": m.get("content", "")}
 1520:                     for m in messages
 1521:                 ],
 1522:                 "response_content": result.content or "[EMPTY_RESPONSE]",
 1523:                 "response_reasoning": result.reasoning or "",
 1524:                 "input_tokens": result.input_tokens,
 1525:                 "output_tokens": result.output_tokens,
 1526:                 "reasoning_tokens": result.reasoning_tokens,
 1527:                 "duration_ms": result.duration_ms,
 1528:                 "cost_usd": _detail_cost,
 1529:             })
 1530: 
 1531:         return result
 1532: 
 1533:     # ------------------------------------------------------------------
 1534:     # Public API: Two modes
 1535:     # ------------------------------------------------------------------
 1536: 
 1537:     async def reason(
 1538:         self,
 1539:         prompt: str,
 1540:         system: str = "",
 1541:         schema: Optional[Type[T]] = None,
 1542:         effort: str = "high",
 1543:         max_tokens: int = 16384,
 1544:         timeout: Optional[float] = None,
 1545:         reasoning_max_tokens: Optional[int] = None,
 1546:         reasoning_exclude: Optional[bool] = None,
 1547:     ) -> LLMResponse:
 1548:         """
 1549:         Analysis/planning call — reasoning ON, returned separately.
 1550: 
 1551:         Use for: query planning, evidence analysis, claim verification,
 1552:         citation checking, gap identification.
 1553: 
 1554:         Reasoning appears in response.reasoning, NOT in response.content.
 1555:         """
 1556:         messages = []
 1557:         if system:
 1558:             messages.append({"role": "system", "content": system})
 1559:         messages.append({"role": "user", "content": prompt})
 1560: 
 1561:         response_format = None
 1562:         if schema:
 1563:             response_format = {
 1564:                 "type": "json_schema",
 1565:                 "json_schema": {
 1566:                     "name": schema.__name__,
 1567:                     "strict": False,
 1568:                     "schema": schema.model_json_schema(),
 1569:                 },
 1570:             }
 1571: 
 1572:         result = await self._call(
